questions.text: What are Steering files in Kiro?

options.label: Workflow guides that direct the agent's behavior for specific tasks
Automations that trigger agent actions when IDE events occur, like saving a file
Installable packages that bundle documentation and MCP servers to expand Kiro
Specification documents that define requirements, design, and tasks for a feature

questions.explanation: Steering files are markdown documents that provide detailed instructions to Kiro on how to execute specific workflows. They direct the agent's behavior toward desired patterns and practices.

questions.text: Steering files can be defined both at the user level (~/.kiro/steering/) and at the workspace level (.kiro/steering/).
questions.explanation: Steering files can exist at two levels: in the user directory (~/.kiro/steering/) for personal settings, and in the workspace (.kiro/steering/) for settings shared with the team via version control.

questions.text: Your team wants Kiro to always follow a specific pattern when creating React components. Which approach is most appropriate?

options.label: Create a Steering file in the workspace with the team's component conventions
Create a Skill with the conventions and ask each developer to activate it manually before creating components
Configure a Hook that rewrites the component to match the standard every time a file is saved
Document conventions in a root README and trust that Kiro reads it automatically in every task

questions.explanation: Steering files in the workspace (.kiro/steering/) are the ideal way to define team conventions. They are loaded automatically and ensure consistency without the need to repeat instructions.

questions.text: Arrange the Steering inclusion types from most automatic to most manual:

options.label: always inclusion - loaded in every interaction automatically
auto inclusion - loaded on demand when Kiro detects relevance from the description
fileMatch inclusion - loaded when file patterns match
manual inclusion - loaded via #name reference by the user

questions.explanation: Steering files in Kiro have four inclusion modes, from most automatic to most manual: 'always' is loaded in every interaction (default); 'auto' uses name/description for Kiro to activate automatically when relevant; 'fileMatch' loads when matching file patterns are opened; and 'manual' requires explicit reference with #name by the user.
